refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting and verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command transmission → vehicle sensor acquisition → telemetry reception at operator console) across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) using instrumented test harness. Collect p95 statistics over minimum 1000 samples per condition. Correlate with operator feedback on control responsiveness and safety margins.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Teleoperation command path shall transmit operator inputs to vehicle with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95)
FR-NGHE-008.2: Vehicle sensor telemetry shall be acquired, encoded, and transmitted to operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95)
FR-NGHE-008.3: Network link shall support bidirectional communication with bounded latency and jitter under agreed conditions
FR-NGHE-008.4: Teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations and network degradation to safety services

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dedicated low-latency network stack with priority queuing, adaptive codec selection, and edge-side buffering to minimize end-to-end latency variance
DP-B: Deploy hybrid architecture with local autonomy fallback and predictive command buffering to mask network latency from operator perception
DP-C: Establish dedicated network slice with QoS guarantees, deterministic routing, and synchronized clock distribution across vehicle and operator console
DP-D: Implement latency monitoring and adaptive control loop with operator feedback to dynamically adjust teleoperation parameters based on network conditions
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A + DP-D: Implement dedicated low-latency network stack with priority queuing and adaptive codec selection (DP-A) combined with continuous latency monitoring and adaptive control loop (DP-D) to maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms under variable network conditions.

potential_sources: Codec selection logic couples to vehicle sensor capabilities and operator display bandwidth
Priority queuing couples to network link provisioning and traffic shaping policies
Adaptive control loop couples to safety services for latency violation reporting
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is acceptable and necessary. DP-A addresses the primary latency reduction mechanism (network stack optimization). DP-D provides feedback control to detect and respond to latency violations, supporting safety criticality. No circular dependencies detected. Decoupling would require separate network and monitoring systems, increasing complexity without safety benefit.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions remain within agreed bounds (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss thresholds)
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle and operator console clocks can be synchronized to within ±10 ms for accurate latency measurement
RISK: Adaptive codec selection may introduce variable latency if codec switching occurs frequently; mitigation: implement hysteresis and predictive switching
RISK: Priority queuing may starve non-critical telemetry if not properly configured; mitigation: enforce minimum bandwidth allocation for safety-critical channels
RISK: Latency monitoring overhead may itself contribute to latency; mitigation: implement lightweight instrumentation with minimal CPU footprint
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify network conditions specification (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, topology) and define latency budget allocation across command path (75 ms), telemetry path (75 ms), and safety reporting (0 ms critical path). Specify codec selection criteria and switching thresholds. Define operator feedback mechanism for latency perception.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates end-to-end teleoperation latency management including command reception, encoding/decoding, network stack optimization, adaptive codec selection, latency monitoring, and safety reporting. Owns the critical path from operator input to vehicle response and back.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from operator console; must be received and queued with minimal latency

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle sensor data (position, orientation, load, camera feed) to operator console; must be encoded and transmitted with bounded latency

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication channel with variable latency and jitter; Teleoperation module manages QoS, priority queuing, and adaptive codec selection on this interface

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Reports latency violations, network degradation, and teleoperation faults to Safety Services for operator notification and emergency response

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end latency management. Command path, telemetry encoding, network stack optimization, and latency monitoring are all core teleoperation functions. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface crosses boundary.

Vehicle module owns sensor acquisition and actuator control but not network communication or latency management. Assigning latency responsibility to Vehicle would scatter network stack logic across multiple modules and create tight coupling between vehicle firmware and network conditions.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and operator notification but not the primary latency reduction mechanism. Assigning latency management to Safety Services would conflate safety monitoring with network optimization, violating separation of concerns.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but not the appropriate module for detailed latency management. Assigning to NGHE Solution would leave implementation responsibility undefined and prevent clear ownership and testing.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: Teleoperation module (primary owner). Vehicle module (secondary owner for sensor acquisition latency). Safety Services (tertiary owner for latency violation reporting).
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module contains the critical latency reduction logic (network stack, codec selection, priority queuing, monitoring). Vehicle module provides sensor data with minimal latency. Safety Services receives and acts on latency alerts. This distribution minimizes diffusion and maintains clear ownership boundaries.
containment_risks: RISK: If network conditions change unexpectedly, Teleoperation module may not have sufficient control authority to adapt; mitigation: define fallback modes and escalation to Safety Services
RISK: Sensor acquisition latency in Vehicle module may exceed budget; mitigation: establish sensor latency SLA and monitor separately

RISK: Operator console latency (display rendering, input processing) is outside NGHE system scope but contributes to perceived latency; mitigation: document end-to-end latency budget including operator console and set expectations
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define latency budget allocation: Vehicle sensor acquisition (≤ 25 ms), Teleoperation encoding/transmission (≤ 50 ms), Network Link (≤ 50 ms), Teleoperation reception/decoding (≤ 25 ms). Establish interface contracts for Tasking (max queue depth, priority levels) and Telemetry (max encoding time, codec options). Define SafetyAlert message format and latency for critical alerts (≤ 10 ms).

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP (low-latency network stack + adaptive monitoring) directly addresses FR requirement for p95 ≤ 150 ms latency. DP-A provides the primary latency reduction mechanism through network optimization. DP-D provides feedback control to detect and respond to latency violations, supporting safety criticality. No logical gaps between FR and DP. Assumptions about network conditions and clock synchronization are reasonable and documented.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: Network stack optimization (DP-A) may require changes to Vehicle module if sensor encoding is not decoupled; mitigation: establish clear interface contract for Telemetry with encoding responsibility in Teleoperation module
DIFFUSION RISK: Adaptive codec selection (DP-A) couples to operator console display capabilities; mitigation: define codec negotiation protocol and fallback options
DIFFUSION RISK: Latency monitoring (DP-D) requires instrumentation across multiple modules; mitigation: implement lightweight telemetry collection in Teleoperation module with minimal overhead
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module provides excellent containment for DP-A and DP-D. Network stack, codec selection, and monitoring logic are naturally co-located. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and minimal. Ownership is clear: Teleoperation module owns latency management end-to-end.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: HIGH: Latency can be measured directly using instrumented test harness with synchronized clocks. p95 metric is well-defined and testable. Network conditions can be simulated or controlled in lab environment. Operator feedback can be collected during field trials.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: HIGH: Teleoperation module has clear responsibility for latency management. Vehicle module has clear responsibility for sensor acquisition. Safety Services has clear responsibility for latency violation reporting. Interface contracts are explicit (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert). No ambiguity in allocation.

recommended_revisions: Refine latency budget allocation across sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.4) with specific targets for each component
Define network conditions specification (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, topology) and establish acceptance criteria for network link
Specify codec selection criteria, switching thresholds, and fallback options
Define operator feedback mechanism for latency perception and control responsiveness
Establish SafetyAlert message format, priority levels, and latency requirements for critical alerts
Document assumptions about clock synchronization, network provisioning, and operator console capabilities

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs to address distinct latency sources (command path, telemetry path, network link, safety reporting). Each sub-FR maps to a specific DP and can be assigned to a module boundary. Decomposition is necessary to establish clear ownership, define interface contracts, and enable independent verification. High safety criticality demands detailed analysis at the next level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: Teleoperation command path shall transmit operator inputs to vehicle with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95)
FR-NGHE-008.2: Vehicle sensor telemetry shall be acquired, encoded, and transmitted to operator with latency ≤ 75 ms (p95)
FR-NGHE-008.3: Network link shall support bidirectional communication with bounded latency and jitter under agreed conditions
FR-NGHE-008.4: Teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations and network degradation to safety services

assumptions: Network conditions remain within agreed bounds (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss thresholds)
Vehicle and operator console clocks can be synchronized to within ±10 ms for accurate latency measurement
Operator console latency (display rendering, input processing) is outside NGHE system scope but contributes to perceived latency
Codec selection can be adapted dynamically without introducing unacceptable latency variance
Priority queuing can be implemented without starving non-critical telemetry channels
Latency monitoring overhead is negligible (< 5 ms) relative to total latency budget
open_risks: RISK: Adaptive codec selection may introduce variable latency if codec switching occurs frequently; mitigation: implement hysteresis and predictive switching
RISK: Priority queuing may starve non-critical telemetry if not properly configured; mitigation: enforce minimum bandwidth allocation for safety-critical channels
RISK: Latency monitoring overhead may itself contribute to latency; mitigation: implement lightweight instrumentation with minimal CPU footprint
RISK: Network conditions may change unexpectedly beyond agreed bounds; mitigation: define fallback modes and escalation to Safety Services
RISK: Sensor acquisition latency in Vehicle module may exceed budget; mitigation: establish sensor latency SLA and monitor separately
RISK: Operator console latency may exceed expectations; mitigation: document end-to-end latency budget including operator console and set expectations
evidence_needed: Network conditions specification (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, topology) and acceptance criteria
Latency budget allocation across command path, telemetry path, and safety reporting
Codec selection criteria, switching thresholds, and fallback options
Operator feedback mechanism for latency perception and control responsiveness
SafetyAlert message format, priority levels, and latency requirements for critical alerts
Clock synchronization method and accuracy specification
Instrumented test harness design and measurement methodology
Field trial plan with operator feedback collection
